name: spherical-geometry

metadata.name: spherical-geometry
metadata.summary: Python based tools for spherical geometry

documentation:
# User documentation

```{image} https://readthedocs.org/projects/spherical-geometry/badge/?version=latest
:alt: Documentation Status
:target: https://spherical-geometry.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest
```

```{image} https://github.com/spacetelescope/spherical_geometry/actions/workflows/spherical_geometry.yml/badge.svg?branch=master
:alt: Spherical Geometry's Github Actions CI Status
:target: https://github.com/spacetelescope/spherical_geometry/actions/workflows/spherical_geometry.yml
```

```{image} https://codecov.io/gh/spacetelescope/spherical_geometry/branch/master/graph/badge.svg
:alt: Spherical Geometry's Coverage Status
:target: https://codecov.io/gh/spacetelescope/spherical_geometry
```

```{image} https://img.shields.io/pypi/v/spherical-geometry
:alt: PyPI
:target: https://pypi.org/project/spherical-geometry/
```

```{image} https://zenodo.org/badge/20937470.svg
:alt: 10.5281/zenodo.10020243
:target: https://zenodo.org/doi/10.5281/zenodo.10020243
```

The `spherical_geometry` library is a Python package for handling spherical
polygons that represent arbitrary regions of the sky.

## Installation

On PyPI:

```
pip install spherical-geometry
```

On conda:

```
conda install -c conda-forge spherical-geometry
```

## Requirements

- Python 3.9 or later
- Numpy 1.23 or later
- astropy 5.0.4 or later
- qd-library 2.3.7 or later (optional: if not available, the
  bundled version will be used). To force using the system-installed
  version, build with `USE_SYSTEM_QD=1 pip install .`.

## Bundled qd-library

Origin: <https://www.davidhbailey.com/dhbsoftware/qd-2.3.24.tar.gz>

- A custom `libqd/include/qd/qd_config.h` is provided to circumvent the need
  to run any configuration scripts. This generalized configuration may not be
  optimized for your system.
- The `spherical_geometry` test suite fail when linked to a system-optimized
  `qd` library, because the tests are written for the general case.

## Coordinate representation

Coordinates in world space are traditionally represented by right
ascension and declination (*ra* and *dec*), or longitude and latitude.
While these representations are convenient, they have discontinuities
at the poles, making operations on them trickier at arbitrary
locations on the sky sphere. Therefore, all internal operations of
this library are done in 3D vector space, where coordinates are
represented as (*x*, *y*, *z*) vectors. The `spherical_geometry.vector` module
contains functions to convert between (*ra*, *dec*) and (*x*, *y*,
*z*) representations.

While any (*x*, *y*, *z*) triple represents a vector and therefore a
location on the sky sphere, a distinction must be made between
normalized coordinates that fall exactly on the unit sphere, and
unnormalized coordinates which do not. A normalized coordinate is
defined as a vector whose length is 1, i.e.:

$$
\sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2} = 1
$$

To prevent unnecessary recomputation, many methods in this library
assume that the vectors passed in are already normalized. If this is
not the case, `spherical_geometry.vector.normalize_vector` can be used to
normalize an array of vectors.

When not working in Cartesian vectors, the library allows the user to
work in either degrees or radians. All methods that require or return
an angular value have a `degrees` keyword argument. When
`degrees` is `True`, these measurements are in degrees, otherwise
they are in radians.

## Spherical polygons

Spherical polygons are arbitrary areas on the sky sphere enclosed by
great circle arcs. They are represented by the
`~spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon` class.

### Representation

The points defining the polygon are available from the
`~polygon.SphericalPolygon.points` property. It is a Nx3 array where
each row is an (*x*, *y*, *z*) vector, normalized. The polygon points
are explicitly closed, i.e., the first and last points are the same.

#### Where is the inside?

The edges of a polygon serve to separate the “inside” from the
“outside” area. On a traditional 2D planar surface, the “inside” is
defined as the finite area and the “outside” is the infinite area.
However, since the surface of a sphere is cyclical, i.e., it wraps
around on itself, the a spherical polygon actually defines two finite
areas. To specify which should be considered the “inside” vs. the
“outside”, the definition of the polygon also has an “inside point”
which is just any point that should be considered inside of the
polygon.

In the following image, the inside point (marked with the red dot)
declares that the area of the polygon is the green region, and not the
white region.

```{image} inside.png
```

The inside point of the the polygon can be obtained from the
`~polygon.SphericalPolygon.inside` property.

#### What is the orientation?

The correctness of several of the algorithms using polygons depends on
a consistent orientation of the points defining it. That is, the points
should have a clockwise order. When creating a new spherical polygon,
the order of the points defining a polygon will be reversed if they are
not in clockwise order. The method `SphericalPolygon.is_clockwise` is
used to est if the points are in clockwise order. It takes two
successive sides and computes the normal vector to the sides. If the
normal vector points inward towards the center of the sphere, the two
sides are counter clockwise. If the normal vector points outward, the
two sides are clockwise. The code determines the orientation by
computing the triple product of the two sides with the vertex of the
the two sides. Summing the triple product over all the sides gives the
predominant orientation of the points in the polygon.

#### Disjoint Polygons

If a polygon is the result of the intersection of polygons, it may be
disjoint. Disjoint polygons are represented as a list of spherical
polygons. The library handles the details of this internally. However,
the user must be aware that several of the properties of polygons are
generators and return the value for a single polygon at a time. To
access all the values of a proeprty, either use a for loop, or coerce
the property to a list. The properties which are generators are:

> - `SphericalPolygon.points`: The points defining each polygon
> - `SphericalPolygon.inside` : The inside point of each polygon

If the intersection of two polygons generates disjoint polygons the
code computes a new interior point for the disjoint polygons.

### Creating spherical polygons

`SphericalPolygon` objects have 5 different constructors:

> - `SphericalPolygon`: Takes an array of (*x*, *y*, *z*) points, or a
>   list of disjoint `SphericalPolygon` instances.
> - `SphericalPolygon.from_radec`: Takes an array of (*ra*, *dec*)
>   points and an inside point.
> - `SphericalPolygon.from_cone`: Creates a polygon from a cone on the
>   sky shere. Takes (*ra*, *dec*, *radius*).
> - `SphericalPolygon.from_wcs`: Creates a polygon from the footprint
>   of a FITS image using its WCS header keywords. Takes a FITS
>   filename or a `astropy.io.fits.Header` object.
> - `SphericalPolygon.convex_hull`: Creates a polygon that is the
>   convex hull of a list of points.

### Operations on Spherical Polygons

Once one has a `SphericalPolygon` object, there are a number of
operations available:

> - `~SphericalPolygon.contains_point`: Determines if the given point
>   is inside the polygon.
> - `~SphericalPolygon.intersects_poly`: Determines if one polygon
>   intersects with another.
> - `~SphericalPolygon.area`: Determine the area of a polygon.
> - `~SphericalPolygon.union` and `~SphericalPolygon.multi_union`:
>   Return a new polygon that is the union of two or more polygons.
> - `~SphericalPolygon.intersection` and
>   `~SphericalPolygon.multi_intersection`: Return a new polygon that
>   is the intersection of two or more polygons.
> - `~SphericalPolygon.overlap`: Determine how much a given polygon
>   overlaps another.
> - `~SphericalPolygon.to_radec`: Convert (*x*, *y*, *z*) points in the
>   polygon to (*ra*, *dec*) points.
> - `~SphericalPolygon.draw`: Plots the polygon using matplotlib’s
>   Basemap toolkit. This feature is rather bare and intended
>   primarily for debugging purposes.

## Great circle arcs

As seen above, great circle arcs are used to define the edges of the
polygon. The `spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc` module contains a number of
functions that are useful for dealing with them.

- `length`: Returns the angular distance between two points on the sphere.
- `intersection`: Returns the intersection point between two great
  circle arcs.
- `intersects`: Determines if two great circle arcs intersect.
- `intersects_point`: Determines if a point is along the great circle
  arc.
- `angle`: Calculate the angle between two great circle arcs.
- `midpoint`: Calculate the midpoint along a great circle arc.

modules.name: spherical_geometry.vector
documentation:
The `spherical_geometry.vector` module contains the basic operations for handling
vectors and converting them to and from other representations.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst

functions.name: spherical_geometry.vector.two_d
documentation: Reshape a one dimensional vector so it has a second dimension

functions.name: spherical_geometry.vector.lonlat_to_vector
documentation:
Converts a location on the unit sphere from longitude and
latitude to an *x*, *y*, *z* vector.

# Parameters

lon, lat : scalars or 1-D arrays

degrees : bool, optional

> If `True`, (default) *lon* and *lat* are in decimal degrees,
> otherwise in radians.

# Returns

x, y, z : tuple of scalars or 1-D arrays of the same length

# Notes

Where longitude is *l* and latitude is *b*:

$$
x = \cos l \cos b

y = \sin l \cos b

z = \sin b
$$

functions.name: spherical_geometry.vector.vector_to_lonlat
documentation:
Converts a vector to longitude and latitude.

# Parameters

x, y, z

: The input vectors

degrees

: If `True` (default) the result is returned in decimal degrees,
  otherwise radians.

# Returns

lon, lat : tuple of scalars or arrays of the same length

# Notes

Where longitude is *l* and latitude is *b*:

$$
l = \arctan2(y, x)

b = \arctan2(z, \sqrt{x^2 + y^2})
$$

functions.name: spherical_geometry.vector.normalize_vector
documentation:
Normalizes a vector so it falls on the unit sphere.

Parameters
----------
xyz : Nx3 array of vectors
    The input vectors

output : Nx3 array of vectors, optional
    The array to store the results in.  If `None`, a new array
    will be created and returned.

Returns
-------
output : Nx3 array of vectors

functions.name: spherical_geometry.vector.rotate_around
documentation:
Rotates the vector (*x*, *y*, *z*) around the arbitrary axis defined by
vector (*u*, *v*, *w*) by *theta*.

It is assumed that both (*x*, *y*, *z*) and (*u*, *v*, *w*) are
already normalized.

Parameters
----------
x, y, z : doubles
    The normalized vector to rotate

u, v, w : doubles
    The normalized vector to rotate around

theta : double, or array of doubles
    The amount to rotate

degrees : bool, optional
    When `True`, *theta* is given in degrees, otherwise radians.

Returns
-------
X, Y, Z : doubles
    The rotated vector

functions.name: spherical_geometry.vector.equal_area_proj
documentation:
Transform the coordinates to a 2-dimensional plane using the
Lambert azimuthal equal-area projection.

# Parameters

points

: The input vectors

# Returns

output : Nx2 array of points

# Notes

$$
X = \sqrt{\frac{2}{1-z}}x
$$

$$
Y = \sqrt{\frac{2}{1-z}}y
$$

modules.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc
documentation:
The `spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc` module contains functions for computing
the length, intersection, angle and midpoint of great circle arcs.

Great circles are circles on the unit sphere whose center is
coincident with the center of the sphere.  Great circle arcs are the
section of those circles between two points on the unit sphere.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc._fast_cross
documentation:
This is a reimplementation of `numpy.cross` that only does 3D x
3D, and is therefore faster since it doesn't need any
conditionals.

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc.intersection
documentation:
Returns the point of intersection between two great circle arcs.
The arcs are defined between the points *AB* and *CD*. Either *A*
and *B* or *C* and *D* may be arrays of points, but not both.

# Parameters

A, B

: Endpoints of the first great circle arc.

C, D

: Endpoints of the second great circle arc.

# Returns

T

: If the given arcs intersect, the intersection is returned. If
  the arcs do not intersect, the triple is set to all NaNs.

# Notes

The basic intersection is computed using linear algebra as follows
[^footnote-1]:

$$
T = \lVert(A × B) × (C × D)\rVert
$$

To determine the correct sign (i.e. hemisphere) of the
intersection, the following four values are computed:

$$
s_1 = ((A × B) × A) \cdot T

s_2 = (B × (A × B)) \cdot T

s_3 = ((C × D) × C) \cdot T

s_4 = (D × (C × D)) \cdot T
$$

For $s_n$, if all positive $T$ is returned as-is. If
all negative, $T$ is multiplied by $-1$. Otherwise
the intersection does not exist and is undefined.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Method explained in an [e-mail](http://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/newsreader/view_thread/276271)
    by Roger Stafford.

<http://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/newsreader/view_thread/276271>

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc.length
documentation:
Returns the angular distance between two points (in vector space)
on the unit sphere.

# Parameters

A, B

: The endpoints of the great circle arc, in vector space.

# Returns

length

: The angular length of the great circle arc in radians.

# Notes

The length is computed using the following:

$$
\Delta = \arccos(A \cdot B)
$$

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc.intersects
documentation:
Returns `True` if the great circle arcs between *AB* and *CD*
intersect.  Either *A* and *B* or *C* and *D* may be arrays of
points, but not both.

Parameters
----------
A, B : (*x*, *y*, *z*) triples or Nx3 arrays of triples
    Endpoints of the first great circle arc.

C, D : (*x*, *y*, *z*) triples or Nx3 arrays of triples
    Endpoints of the second great circle arc.

Returns
-------
intersects : bool or array of bool
    If the given arcs intersect, the intersection is returned as
    `True`.

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc.intersects_point
documentation:
Returns True if point C is along the great circle arc *AB*.

Parameters
----------
A, B : (*x*, *y*, *z*) triples or Nx3 arrays of triples
    Endpoints of the great circle arc.

C : (*x*, *y*, *z*) triples or array of triples of points

Returns
-------
intersects : bool or array of bool
    If the point is on the line, returns `True`.

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc.angle
documentation:
Returns the angle at *B* between *AB* and *BC*.

# Parameters

A, B, C

: Points on sphere.

# Returns

angle

: The angle at *B* between *AB* and *BC*, in range 0 to 2π.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Miller, Robert D. Computing the area of a spherical
    polygon. Graphics Gems IV. 1994. Academic Press.

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc.midpoint
documentation:
Returns the midpoint on the great circle arc between *A* and *B*.

Parameters
----------
A, B : (*x*, *y*, *z*) triples or Nx3 arrays of triples
    The endpoints of the great circle arc.  It is assumed that
    these points are already normalized.

Returns
-------
midpoint : (*x*, *y*, *z*) triple or Nx3 arrays of triples
    The midpoint between *A* and *B*, normalized on the unit
    sphere.

functions.name: spherical_geometry.great_circle_arc.interpolate
documentation:
Interpolate along the great circle arc.

# Parameters

A, B

: The endpoints of the great circle arc. It is assumed thats
  these points are already normalized.

steps

: The number of interpolation steps

# Returns

array

: The points interpolated along the great circle arc

# Notes

This uses Slerp interpolation where *Ω* is the angle subtended by
the arc, and *t* is the parameter 0 \<= *t* \<= 1.

$$
\frac{\sin((1 - t)\Omega)}{\sin \Omega}A + \frac{\sin(t \Omega)}{\sin \Omega}B
$$

modules.name: spherical_geometry.graph
documentation: This contains the code that does the actual unioning of regions.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst

functions.name: spherical_geometry.graph.node_order
documentation: The following two functions are called by sorted to provide a consistent
ordering of nodes and edges retrieved from the graph, since values are
retrieved from sets in an order that varies from run to run

classes.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
polygons : sequence of `~spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon` instances
    Build a graph from this initial set of polygons.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.add_polygons
documentation:
Add more polygons to the graph.

:::{note}
Must be called before `union` or `intersection`.
:::

# Parameters

polygons

: Set of polygons to add to the graph

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.add_polygon
documentation:
Add a single polygon to the graph.

:::{note}
Must be called before `union` or `intersection`.
:::

# Parameters

polygon

: Polygon to add to the graph

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._add_node
documentation:
Add a node to the graph.  It will be disconnected until used
in a call to `_add_edge`.

Parameters
----------
point : 3-sequence (*x*, *y*, *z*) coordinate

source_polygon : `~spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon` instance, optional
    The polygon this node came from.  Used for bookkeeping.

Returns
-------
node : `~Graph.Node` instance
    The new node

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_node
documentation:
Removes a node and all of the edges that touch it.

:::{note}
It is assumed that *Node* is already a part of the graph.
:::

# Parameters

node : `~Graph.Node` instance

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._add_edge
documentation:
Add an edge between two nodes.

:::{note}
It is assumed both nodes already belong to the graph.
:::

# Parameters

A, B : `~Graph.Node` instances

source_polygons

: The polygon(s) this edge came from. Used for bookkeeping.

# Returns

edge

: The new edge

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_edge
documentation:
Remove an edge from the graph. The nodes it points to remain intact.

:::{note}
It is assumed that *edge* is already a part of the graph.
:::

# Parameters

edge : `~Graph.Edge` instance

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._split_edge
documentation:
Splits an `~Graph.Edge` *edge* at `~Graph.Node` *node*, removing
*edge* and replacing it with two new `~Graph.Edge` instances.
It is intended that *E* is along the original edge, but that is
not enforced.

Parameters
----------
edge : `~Graph.Edge` instance
    The edge to split

node : `~Graph.Node` instance
    The node to insert

Returns
-------
edgeA, edgeB : `~Graph.Edge` instances
    The two new edges on either side of *node*.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._sanity_check
documentation:
For debugging purposes: assert that edges and nodes are
connected to each other correctly and there are no orphaned
edges or nodes.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.union
documentation:
Once all of the polygons have been added to the graph,
join the polygons together.

Returns
-------
points : Nx3 array of (*x*, *y*, *z*) points
    This is a list of points outlining the union of the
    polygons that were given to the constructor.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.intersection
documentation:
Once all of the polygons have been added to the graph,
calculate the intersection.

Returns
-------
points : Nx3 array of (*x*, *y*, *z*) points
    This is a list of points outlining the intersection of the
    polygons that were given to the constructor.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.disjoint_polygons
documentation:
Convert a graph containing cut lines and self intersections
into a list of disjoint polygons

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_cut_lines
documentation:
Removes any cutlines that may already have existed in the
input polygons.  This is so any cutlines in the final result
will be optimized to be as short as possible and won't
intersect each other.

This works by finding coincident edges that are reverse to
each other, and then splicing around them.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._find_all_intersections
documentation:
Find all the intersecting edges in the graph.  For each
intersecting pair, four new edges are created around the
intersection point.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_interior_edges
documentation:
Removes any nodes that are contained inside other polygons.
What's left is the (possibly disjunct) outline.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_exterior_edges
documentation:
Removes any edges that are not contained in all of the source
polygons.  What's left is the (possibly disjunct) outline.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_degenerate_edges
documentation: Remove edges where both endpoints are the same point

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_3ary_edges
documentation:
Remove edges between pairs of nodes that have odd numbers of
edges.  This removes triangles that can't be traced.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._remove_orphaned_nodes
documentation: Remove nodes with fewer than 2 edges.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._contains_inside_point
documentation:
Check if the polygons in the graph all contain
the interior point of a polygon

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._trace_polygons
documentation:
Given a graph that has had cutlines removed and all
intersections found, traces it to find a list of
disjoint polygons

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph._trace
documentation:
Given a graph that has had cutlines removed and all
intersections found, traces it to find a resulting single
polygon.

inner_classes.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.Node

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.Node.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
point : 3-sequence (*x*, *y*, *z*) coordinate

source_polygon : `~spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon` instance, optional
    The polygon(s) this node came from.  Used for bookkeeping.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.Node.equals
documentation:
Returns `True` if the location of this and the *other*
`~Graph.Node` are the same.

Parameters
----------
other : `~Graph.Node` instance
    The other node.

thres : float
    If difference is smaller than this, points are equal.
    The default value of 2e-8 radians is set based on
    empirical test cases. Relative threshold based on
    the actual sizes of polygons is not implemented.

A `~Graph.Node` represents a single point, connected by an arbitrary
number of `~Graph.Edge` objects to other `~Graph.Node` objects.

inner_classes.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.Edge

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.Edge.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
A, B : `~Graph.Node` instances

source_polygon : `~spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon` instance, optional
    The polygon this edge came from.  Used for bookkeeping.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.Edge.follow
documentation:
Follow along the edge from the given *node* to the other
node.

Parameters
----------
node : `~Graph.Node` instance

Returns
-------
other : `~Graph.Node` instance

methods.name: spherical_geometry.graph.Graph.Edge.equals
documentation:
Returns `True` if the other edge is between the same two nodes.

Parameters
----------
other : `~Graph.Edge` instance

Returns
-------
equals : bool

An `~Graph.Edge` represents a connection between exactly two
`~Graph.Node` objects.  This `~Graph.Edge` class has no direction.

A graph of nodes connected by edges. The graph is used to build
unions between polygons.

:::{note}
This class is not meant to be used directly. Instead, use
`~spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.union` and
`~spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.intersection`.
:::

modules.name: spherical_geometry.polygon
documentation:
The `spherical_geometry.polygon` module defines the `SphericalPolygon` class for
managing polygons on the unit sphere.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-
Licensed under a 3-clause BSD style license - see LICENSE.rst

classes.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
points : An Nx3 array of (*x*, *y*, *z*) triples in vector space
    These points define the boundary of the polygon.

    It may contain zero points, in which it defines the null
    polygon.  It may not contain one, two or three points.
    Four points are needed to define a triangle, since the
    polygon must be closed.

inside : An (*x*, *y*, *z*) triple, optional
    This point must be inside the polygon.  If not provided, an
    interior point will be calculated

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.__iter__
documentation:
Iterate over all base polygons that make up this multi-polygon
set.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.points
documentation:
The points defining the polygon.  It is an Nx3 array of
(*x*, *y*, *z*) vectors.  The polygon will be explicitly
closed, i.e., the first and last points are the same.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.inside
documentation: Get the inside point of the polygon.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.is_clockwise
documentation:
Return True if the points in this polygon are in clockwise order.

The normal vector to the two arcs containing a vertes points outward
from the sphere if the angle is clockwise and inward if the angle is
counter-clockwise. The sign of the inner product of the normal vector
with the vertex tells you this. The polygon is ordered clockwise if
the vertices are predominantly clockwise and counter-clockwise if
the reverse.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.to_lonlat
documentation:
Convert `SingleSphericalPolygon` footprint to longitude and latitutde.

Returns
-------
lon, lat : list of float
    List of *lon* and *lat* in degrees corresponding
    to `points`.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.from_lonlat
documentation:
Create a new `SingleSphericalPolygon` from a list of
(*longitude*, *latitude*) points.

Parameters
----------
lon, lat : 1-D arrays of the same length
    The vertices of the polygon in longitude and
    latitude.

center : (*lon*, *lat*) pair, optional
    A point inside of the polygon to define its inside.

degrees : bool, optional
    If `True`, (default) *lon* and *lat* are in decimal degrees,
    otherwise in radians.

Returns
-------
polygon : `SingleSphericalPolygon` object

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.from_cone
documentation:
Create a new `SingleSphericalPolygon` from a cone (otherwise known
as a "small circle") defined using (*lon*, *lat*, *radius*).

The cone is not represented as an ideal circle on the sphere,
but as a series of great circle arcs.  The resolution of this
conversion can be controlled using the *steps* parameter.

Parameters
----------
lon, lat : float scalars
    This defines the center of the cone

radius : float scalar
    The radius of the cone

degrees : bool, optional
    If `True`, (default) *lon*, *lat* and *radius* are in
    decimal degrees, otherwise in radians.

steps : int, optional
    The number of steps to use when converting the small
    circle to a polygon.

Returns
-------
polygon : `SingleSphericalPolygon` object

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.from_wcs
documentation:
Create a new `SingleSphericalPolygon` from the footprint of a FITS
WCS specification.

This method requires having `astropy <http://astropy.org>`__
installed.

Parameters
----------
fitspath : path to a FITS file, `astropy.io.fits.Header`, or `astropy.wcs.WCS`
    Refers to a FITS header containing a WCS specification.

steps : int, optional
    The number of steps along each edge to convert into
    polygon edges.

Returns
-------
polygon : `SingleSphericalPolygon` object

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.convex_hull
documentation:
Create a new `SingleSphericalPolygon` from the convex hull of a
list of points using the Graham Scan algorithm

Parameters
----------
points: A list of points on the unit sphere

Returns
-------
polygon : `SingleSphericalPolygon` object

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.invert_polygon
documentation: Compute the inverse (complement) of a single polygon

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.contains_point
documentation:
Determines if this `SingleSphericalPolygon` contains a given point.

Parameters
----------
point : an (*x*, *y*, *z*) triple
    The point to test.

Returns
-------
contains : bool
    Returns `True` if the polygon contains the given *point*.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.contains_lonlat
documentation:
Determines if this `SingleSphericalPolygon` contains a given
 longitude and latitude.

 Parameters
 ----------
 lon, lat: Longitude and latitude. Must be scalars.

 degrees : bool, optional

If `True`, (default) *lon* and *lat* are in decimal degrees,
otherwise in radians.

 Returns
 -------
 contains : bool
     Returns `True` if the polygon contains the given *point*.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.intersects_poly
documentation:
Determines if this `SingleSphericalPolygon` intersects another
`SingleSphericalPolygon`.

This method is much faster than actually computing the
intersection region between two polygons.

# Parameters

other : `SingleSphericalPolygon`

# Returns

intersects

: Returns `True` if this polygon intersects the *other*
  polygon.

# Notes

The algorithm proceeds as follows:

> 1. Determine if any single point of one polygon is contained
>    within the other.
>
> 2. Deal with the case where only the edges overlap as in:
>
>    ```
>    :       o---------o
>    :  o----+---------+----o
>    :  |    |         |    |
>    :  o----+---------+----o
>    :       o---------o
>    ```
>
>    In this case, an edge from one polygon must cross an
>    edge from the other polygon.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.intersects_arc
documentation:
Determines if this `SingleSphericalPolygon` intersects or contains
the given arc.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.contains_arc
documentation:
Returns `True` if the polygon fully encloses the arc given by a
and b.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.area
documentation:
Returns the area of the polygon on the unit sphere, in
steradians.

The area is computed using a generalization of Girard's Theorem.

if $\theta$ is the sum of the internal angles of the
polygon, and *n* is the number of vertices, the area is:

$$
S = \theta - (n - 2) \pi
$$

The area can be negative if the points on the polygon are ordered
counter-clockwise. Take the absolute value if that is not desired.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.union
documentation:
Return a new `SphericalPolygon` that is the union of *self*
and *other*.

# Parameters

other : `SingleSphericalPolygon`

# Returns

polygon : `SphericalPolygon` object

# See also

SphericalPolygon.multi_union

# Notes

For implementation details, see the
{mod}`~spherical_geometry.graph` module.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon._find_new_inside
documentation:
Finds an acceptable inside point inside of *points* that is
also inside of *polygons*.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon._find_new_outside
documentation: Finds an acceptable point outside of the polygon

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.intersection
documentation:
Return a new `SphericalPolygon` that is the intersection of
*self* and *other*.

If the intersection is empty, a `SphericalPolygon` with zero
subpolyons will be returned.

# Parameters

other : `SingleSphericalPolygon`

# Returns

polygon : `SphericalPolygon` object

# See also

SphericalPolygon.multi_union

# Notes

For implementation details, see the
{mod}`~spherical_geometry.graph` module.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.overlap
documentation:
Returns the fraction of *self* that is overlapped by *other*.

Let *self* be *a* and *other* be *b*, then the overlap is
defined as:

$$
\frac{S_{a \cap b}}{S_a}
$$

# Parameters

other : `SingleSphericalPolygon`

# Returns

frac

: The fraction of *self* that is overlapped by *other*.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SingleSphericalPolygon.draw
documentation:
Draws the polygon in a matplotlib.Basemap axes.

Parameters
----------
m : Basemap axes object

**plot_args : Any plot arguments to pass to basemap

Polygons are represented by both a set of points (in Cartesian
(*x*, *y*, *z*) normalized on the unit sphere), and an inside
point.  The inside point is necessary, because both the inside and
outside of the polygon are finite areas on the great sphere, and
therefore we need a way of specifying which is which.

classes.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
init : object
    May be either:
       - A list of disjoint `SphericalPolygon` objects.

       - An Nx3 array of (*x*, *y*, *z*) triples in Cartesian
         space.  These points define the boundary of the
         polygon.

         It may contain zero points, in which it defines the
         null polygon.  It may not contain one or two points.

inside : An (*x*, *y*, *z*) triple, optional
    If *init* is an array of points, this point must be inside
    the polygon.  If it is not provided, one will be created.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.__iter__
documentation:
Iterate over all base polygons that make up this multi-polygon
set.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.points
documentation:
The points defining the polygons.  It is an iterator over
disjoint closed polygons, where each element is an Nx3 array
of (*x*, *y*, *z*) vectors.  Each polygon is explicitly
closed, i.e., the first and last points are the same.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.inside
documentation: Iterate over the inside point of each of the polygons.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.polygons
documentation:
Get a sequence of all of the subpolygons.  Each subpolygon may
itself have subpolygons.  To get a flattened sequence of all
base polygons, use `iter_polygons_flat`.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.is_clockwise
documentation: Return True if all subpolygons are clockwise

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.self_intersect
documentation:
Return true if the path defined by a list of points
intersects itself

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.to_lonlat
documentation:
Convert the `SphericalPolygon` footprint to longitude and latitude
coordinates.

Returns
-------
polyons : iterator
    Each element in the iterator is a tuple of the form (*lon*,
    *lat*), where each is an array of points.

The cone is not represented as an ideal circle on the sphere,

conversion can be controlled using the *steps* parameter.

    This defines the center of the cone

degrees : bool, optional

steps : int, optional

Returns

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.area
documentation:
Returns the area of the polygon on the unit sphere in
steradians.

The area is computed using a generalization of Girard's Theorem.

if $\theta$ is the sum of the internal angles of the
polygon, and *n* is the number of vertices, the area is:

$$
S = \theta - (n - 2) \pi

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.union
documentation:
Return a new `SphericalPolygon` that is the union of *self*
and *other*.

# Parameters

other : `SphericalPolygon`

# Returns

polygon : `SphericalPolygon` object

# See also

multi_union

# Notes

For implementation details, see the {mod}`~spherical_geometry.graph`
module.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.multi_union
documentation:
Return a new `SphericalPolygon` that is the union of all of the
polygons in *polygons*. Currently this implementation exhibits
exponential time behavior and becomes practically unusable when
dealing with on the order of 40 or more polygons.

Also, current implementation struggles when some of the input polygons
are nearly identical. As a workaround, this method pre-filters
input polygons and excludes those nearly the same as some other input
polygon. Two poligons treated as the same polygon if their vertices
(`x`, `y`, and `z` cordinates on a unit sphere) differ by less
than `5e-9`. This is equivalent to polygon vertices being separated
by less than 0.0015 arcsec on the sky or by less than `2 mm` on Earth
(at average Earth radius).

# Parameters

polygons : sequence of `SphericalPolygon`

# Returns

polygon : `SphericalPolygon` object

# See also

union

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.intersection
documentation:
Return a new `SphericalPolygon` that is the intersection of
*self* and *other*.

If the intersection is empty, a `SphericalPolygon` with zero
subpolygons will be returned.

# Parameters

other : `SphericalPolygon`

# Returns

polygon : `SphericalPolygon` object

# Notes

For implementation details, see the
{mod}`~spherical_geometry.graph` module.

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.multi_intersection
documentation:
Return a new `SphericalPolygon` that is the intersection of
all of the polygons in *polygons*.

Parameters
----------
polygons : sequence of `SphericalPolygon`

Returns
-------
polygon : `SphericalPolygon` object

methods.name: spherical_geometry.polygon.SphericalPolygon.draw
documentation:
Draws the polygon in a matplotlib.Basemap axes.

Parameters
----------
m : Basemap axes object

**plot_args : Any plot arguments to pass to basemap

Polygons are represented by both a set of points (in Cartesian
(*x*, *y*, *z*) normalized on the unit sphere), and an inside
point.  The inside point is necessary, because both the inside and
outside of the polygon are finite areas on the great sphere, and
therefore we need a way of specifying which is which.

This class contains a list of disjoint closed polygons.
